EUROPE’s gas storage sites have increased their fill rate by almost 16 percentage points since the winter heating season ended on March 31. Refilling has been slighter slower than usual with an average increase nearer 19 points over the previous ten years. Despite low inventories when last winter ended, utilities and other merchants have delayed refilling as much as possible in the expectation LNG tanker flows through the Strait of Hormuz will resume and prices retreat later in the year:

China’s oil stockpiling strategy vindicated by Hormuz crisis

China has cut crude oil imports and refinery processing volumes to multi-year lows in response to the war between the United States and Iran and the interruption of oil imports via the Strait of Hormuz.

Petroleum planners appear to have suspended crude imports into strategic storage and have likely run down commercial stocks of both crude and refined products while restricting fuel exports.

The enormous inventories of crude and refined fuels China accumulated before the war have provided significant flexibility and allowed it to step back from global markets temporarily ...

Consumer prices rose 0.5% in May, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported Wednesday, lifting annual inflation to 4.2% from 3.8%—the first 4%-handle increase in three years.

But the heat was mostly contained in energy: It accounted for more than 60% of the monthly damage, with gasoline jumping 7% from April and 40.5% over the year as the war in Iran chokes the Strait of Hormuz and drives oil prices higher.

If you strip out food and energy from the “core” CPI, it rose just 0.2% in May and 2.9% over the year, softer than expected and suggesting that the spillover was relatively limited. That’s the number the Fed actually watches, and it isn’t flashing the overheating signs that could force its hand.

Overheating was the fear over this print, not just from the Iran war but from AI. Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee has warned that the AI boom could heat the economy before it delivers any productivity gains, as the costs of the build-out grow. China’s economy showed a version of this overnight, with wholesale inflation near a four-year high on AI-driven demand. But May’s consumer data doesn’t show this yet; the hot part is still the oil shock.

Yet an inflation rate above 4% makes a rate cut harder for new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh to justify, especially after a surprisingly strong May jobs report. The risk now is that the oil shock, like the previous supply shocks that have battered the economy since the pandemic, proves to be sticky.

It’s an awkward print for Warsh in particular. He was confirmed in May in the closest Fed chair vote in modern history, and critics accuse him of being installed largely because Trump expects him to cut rates. Warsh, for his part, has argued that productivity gains from AI will allow the economy to grow faster without spurring inflation, thereby enabling the Fed to lower borrowing costs. 

But the American public is showing signs of fatigue with five years of above-2% inflation. Consumer sentiment fell to a record low in May, marking its third straight monthly drop, with 57% of Americans reporting that high prices are eroding their finances. Wednesday’s print showed that wages rose just 3.4% over the year, meaning that 4.2% inflation is beginning to outpace workers’ pay gains as prices rise in gas, electricity, food, and medical care. 

The market has gotten the message. After May’s strong jobs report and Goldman Sachs pulling its 2026 rate-cut forecast, traders began pricing out a rate cut and pricing in a hike. As of Wednesday, the CME FedWatch tool priced a 63% chance of a quarter-point increase by October, a near-total reversal from the cut consensus that dominated Wall Street weeks ago.

Jeffrey Roach, chief economist at LPL Financial, expects the central bank to “remain on hold while removing any bias toward additional easing,” basically stripping out the signal that cuts are coming.

Goldman Slashes 2027 Brent Outlook to $80

Goldman Sachs cut its average 2027 Brent crude forecast to $80/bbl, from $85, pointing to robust non-OPEC supply growth and structural demand weakness, led by China’s EV push and efficiency gains.

It held Q4 2026 at $90/bbl, noting the Hormuz disruption’s price impact has been milder than expected thanks to softer demand.

Goldman is doubling down on the fundamentals over geopolitics thesis.

Even with ongoing Strait risks, they see supply flooding in and demand softening enough to cap longer-term prices.

Goldman Sachs just cut its 2027 Brent crude estimate to $80 per barrel.

The revision is based on two factors: stronger supply from non-OPEC producers and structurally weaker demand from China.

Goldman estimates that over 10% of China's demand decline becomes permanent as the country diversifies its energy mix away from oil.

This matters because China has been the marginal buyer driving global oil demand growth for two decades. A structural shift changes the long-term calculus for crude.

Brent recently dropped 6.2% in a single session. WTI fell 5.9%. These moves may reflect the market repricing not just short-term supply concerns, but longer-term demand uncertainty.

The contrast is notable: short-term volatility is getting headlines, but the bigger story is a major bank revising its multi-year outlook lower based on permanent demand destruction.

Worth watching whether other banks follow with similar revisions.

Short-term volatility, but the real story is structural demand - watch China.

$USO #Oil #GoldmanSachs #CrudeOil #Markets #FinTok #Investing

🛢️ Goldman Sachs Just Trimmed Its 2027 Brent Oil Outlook, What This Means for Energy Markets

In a notable shift, Goldman Sachs has lowered its forecast for the average 2027 Brent crude oil price to $80 per barrel, down from a previous estimate of $85.

This revision reflects a more cautious long-term view, driven primarily by expectations of robust global supply growth, particularly from non-OPEC producers, meeting softer-than-anticipated demand. Factors like moderating economic growth in key consuming regions, efficiency gains, and the ongoing energy transition appear to be weighing on the demand side, while ample inventories and renewed production capacity ease earlier tightness concerns.

Why This Matters:
For Producers & Energy Companies: A lower price trajectory could pressure margins, especially for higher-cost operators. It signals a return toward structural balance rather than prolonged scarcity, potentially capping upside for oil equities and related sectors into 2027.

For Investors: This aligns with broader market sentiment from institutions like the EIA, which also sees prices easing toward the high-$70s/low-$80s range as supply responses kick in. However, geopolitical risks (e.g., ongoing tensions in key chokepoints) remain wild cards that could spark short-term volatility.

Macro Implications: Cheaper oil supports consumer spending and helps tame inflation, but it may challenge oil-exporting economies and investment in new upstream projects.

Oil markets are notoriously cyclical, today’s bearish revisions can quickly flip with any supply disruption or demand surprise. Goldman’s move highlights how rapidly forecasts evolve amid shifting fundamentals.

What’s your take? 
Will we see $80 Brent hold as the new normal in 2027, or do you expect volatility to push prices higher on unexpected catalysts? Drop your thoughts below 👇

#Oil #BrentCrude #EnergyMarkets #GoldmanSachs #Commodities #Investing

GOLDMAN SACHS CUT ITS 2027 BRENT CRUDE OUTLOOK TO $80/BBL, POINTING TO RISING SUPPLY AND SOFTER DEMAND AS KEY DRIVERS. ...

...anything that gets shipped, because $90 oil doesn't stay at the refinery. It moves through everything.

Goldman isn't panicking and isn't revising down. That's the signal. In a week where oil has been pulled in every direction by trade war fears and demand uncertainty, the bank looked at all of it and kept its number exactly where it was.

At $90 a barrel, petrol prices stay elevated. Not crisis-level, but well above the sub-$70 lows we saw earlier this year. The relief at the pump that drivers briefly felt doesn't fully come back.

The hidden pressure is on industries that run on diesel and freight. Supermarkets, logistics companies, manufacturers. Their input costs stay high. That feeds into prices on the shelf, quietly, over months.

Goldman's refusal to cut the forecast is essentially a bet that the world keeps consuming oil faster than it can be replaced by alternatives. That bet has been right for two years running.

CME Group expanding 24/7 trading for WTI crude oil and gold, enabling round-the-clock market access for both commodities.

🟢 MARKET UPDATE — FRIDAY, JUNE 12, 2026

The market woke up today believing the Middle East war may finally be approaching an off-ramp.

🔸 US futures are higher.

🔸 Oil prices are lower.

🔸 SpaceX begins trading today.

🔸 But inflation fears remain stubbornly alive.

And that is why today’s story is more complicated than a simple relief rally.

🟢 PEACE HOPES SEND MARKETS HIGHER

The biggest development overnight was growing optimism that the US and Iran may be close to a deal.

Reports suggest the draft agreement could:

🔸 Reopen the Strait of Hormuz

🔸 Halt hostilities

🔸 Ease some oil sanctions

🔸 Release part of Iran’s frozen funds

Markets immediately reacted.

At the time of writing:

🔸 Dow futures: +248 points (+0.49%)

🔸 S&P 500 futures: +0.27%

🔸 Nasdaq futures: +0.02%

🔸 DXY Dollar Index: 99.85

Translation:

Investors are betting that fewer missiles means cheaper oil, lower inflation and less economic damage.

🟢 OIL DROPS AS HORMUZ HOPES RETURN

Oil was one of the biggest movers.

Brent crude briefly fell below $90.

WTI dropped toward $84.

Why?

Because the Strait of Hormuz carries roughly one-fifth of global oil supplies.

If the waterway gradually reopens:

🔸 More oil can reach global markets

🔸 Energy shortages ease

🔸 Inflation pressure falls

🔸 Central banks get breathing room

But there is a catch.

Iranian media later reported that Hormuz activity may not immediately return to pre-war levels.

Translation:

The market is pricing in peace.

The actual oil market is still waiting for proof.

🟢 SPACEX TAKES CENTRE STAGE TODAY

The other huge story is SpaceX.

The company priced its IPO at:

🔸 $135 per share

🔸 Raised approximately $75 billion

🔸 Largest IPO in history

Trading is expected later today.

This is not just a SpaceX story.

It is a test of investor appetite for:

🔸 AI

🔸 Space infrastructure

🔸 Future technologies

🔸 High-growth companies

Translation:

If SpaceX performs strongly, it could inject fresh energy into the broader AI and technology rally.

If demand disappoints, it may cool some of the enthusiasm that has driven markets higher.

🟢 WALL STREET’S FEAR IS FADING

The VIX, often called Wall Street’s fear gauge, has fallen back below 20.

That suggests investors are becoming less worried about:

🔸 A wider Middle East war

🔸 An oil shock

🔸 A global recession

For now.

Translation:

Markets have moved from panic mode back into cautious optimism.

🟢 THE BOND MARKET IS STILL WARNING INVESTORS

While stocks are celebrating, the bond market remains sceptical.

US Treasury yields remain elevated:

🔸 2-Year Yield: 4.09%

🔸 10-Year Yield: 4.48%

🔸 30-Year Yield: 4.98%

Why aren’t yields falling more?

Because inflation remains a problem.

Many economists still expect US inflation to remain near 4% for much of 2026.

Translation:

The market believes peace may lower oil prices.

But it does not yet believe inflation has been defeated.

🟢 FED RATE CUTS ARE BEING PUSHED FURTHER AWAY

Bloomberg reports economists now expect the Federal Reserve to keep interest rates unchanged until around the middle of next year.

Simple explanation:

A few months ago investors expected rate cuts.

Now many are wondering whether rates will stay high much longer.

Why?

Because inflation keeps proving more stubborn than expected.

Translation:

Even if the war cools down, borrowing costs may remain painful.

🟢 JAPAN IS ABOUT TO DO SOMETHING IT HASN’T DONE IN 31 YEARS

One of the most important stories almost nobody is talking about:

The Bank of Japan is expected to raise interest rates to 1%.

That would be the highest level since 1995.

Simple explanation:

Japan spent decades with ultra-low interest rates.

Now inflation is forcing Japanese policymakers to join the global fight against rising prices.

Why it matters:

Japan is one of the largest sources of global investment capital.

Higher Japanese rates can:

🔸 Strengthen the yen

🔸 Reduce global liquidity

🔸 Increase borrowing costs worldwide

🔸 Affect stocks, bonds and cryptocurrencies

Think of Japan as one of the world’s biggest reservoirs of cheap money.

That reservoir is slowly being drained.

🟢 SOUTH KOREA IS FACING THE SAME PROBLEM

South Korea’s central bank is also signalling rate hikes.

Inflation has risen to 3.1%.

Its target is only 2%.

Translation:

Central banks across Asia are becoming more worried about inflation than economic growth.

That is usually not a great environment for risk assets.

🟢 CHINA JUST SENT A QUIETLY POSITIVE SIGNAL

Bloomberg reports China’s credit growth rebounded in May.

Simple explanation:

Credit is the fuel that powers economic activity.

When banks lend more:

🔸 Businesses invest

🔸 Consumers spend

🔸 Construction projects continue

🔸 Growth improves

China’s credit expansion was stronger than expected.

Translation:

The world’s second-largest economy may be stabilising after a weak patch.

That is good news for global growth and commodity demand.

🟢 INDIA IS FEELING THE COST OF THE WAR

India may allow its budget deficit to rise from 4.3% to 4.8% of GDP.

Simple explanation:

The government may spend more money than planned.

Why?

Because:

🔸 Oil prices surged

🔸 Fuel subsidies became more expensive

🔸 Fertiliser costs increased

🔸 Energy imports became more costly

India imports about 90% of its oil.

Translation:

When oil rises, India feels the pain almost immediately.

🟢 GOLD IS LOSING SOME OF ITS FEAR PREMIUM

Gold demand improved in India as prices fell.

China’s gold premiums also eased.

Meanwhile:

🔸 China’s central bank increased gold reserves for a 19th consecutive month.

Translation:

Short-term traders are taking profits.

Long-term central banks are still accumulating.

Those are very different signals.

🟢 THE WORLD ECONOMY IS STILL SLOWING

The World Bank now expects global growth of just 2.5% in 2026.

It specifically cited the Iran war as a major reason.

Meanwhile:

🔸 Britain’s economy contracted 0.1% in April

🔸 Energy costs hurt consumers

🔸 Businesses became more cautious

🔸 Growth momentum weakened

Translation:

Even if peace arrives tomorrow, some economic damage has already been done.

🟢 NEXT BIG EVENT: MICHIGAN CONSUMER SENTIMENT

Coming at 10:00 AM ET.

Forecast: 46

Previous: 44.8

Simple explanation:

This measures how ordinary Americans feel about the economy.

A stronger reading suggests:

🔸 Consumers are feeling better

🔸 Spending may remain strong

🔸 Growth may hold up

A weaker reading suggests the opposite.

Markets will be watching closely.

🟢 TRADER SUMMARY

Today’s market is being pulled by two powerful forces:

🔸 Falling geopolitical risk → bullish

🔸 Persistent inflation → bearish

Investors are celebrating the possibility of peace.

Bond markets are still worried about inflation.

And today’s SpaceX debut could become the next major test for market sentiment.

For now, markets are choosing hope.

The question is whether reality catches up.

Sources: MarketWatch, Barron’s, Financial Times, Reuters, Bloomberg, WSJ, Al Jazeera English, The Economist, TradingView.

#Markets #Investing #SpaceX

Dow futures up 0.33% as Trump signals Iran deal imminent; WTI crude crashes 2.93% on de-escalation hopes. Silver surges 3.93%, gold +2.37%. Space stocks soar with five names added to Nasdaq-100 ahead of SpaceX IPO at $135/share. Chip stocks mixed despite tech sector strength.

🚨 BREAKING: U.S. forces intercept Iranian attack drones over Strait of Hormuz.  U.S. forces shot down two Iranian one way attack drones overnight after Tehran targeted commercial ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz. This follows a massive wave of rapid regional escalations over the last 72 hours.  

U.S. forces struck 3 tankers for violating its maritime blockade on Iran, including a strike on the Palau flagged Settebello that left 3 Indian mariners dead.
The IRGC retaliated and launched drone and missile barrages at U.S. bases in Bahrain, Kuwait, and Jordan. Falling shrapnel caused property damage in Manama, and Kuwait temporarily shut its airspace.
Prior to the drone intercepts, the U.S. completed overnight airstrikes inside Iran, targeting air defense and communication sites in Karaj and Bandar Abbas.
While CENTCOM is maintaining commercial traffic flow, Iran has declared the Strait completely closed to U.S. linked vessels. Brent crude has jumped to ~$95/barrel.  Once again the talks of a Deal seem to be FALSE.  Lock in.

𝐓𝐑𝐔𝐌𝐏 𝐂𝐀𝐍𝐂𝐄𝐋𝐒 𝐓𝐇𝐄 𝐒𝐓𝐑𝐈𝐊𝐄𝐒 — 𝐅𝐎𝐔𝐑 𝐂-𝟏𝟕𝐒 𝐇𝐄𝐀𝐃 𝐅𝐎𝐑 𝐄𝐔𝐑𝐎𝐏𝐄 𝐀𝐒 𝐕𝐀𝐍𝐂𝐄 𝐏𝐑𝐄𝐏𝐀𝐑𝐄𝐒 𝐓𝐎 𝐒𝐈𝐆𝐍 𝐈𝐑𝐀𝐍 𝐌𝐎𝐔

“𝘞𝘦’𝘭𝘭 𝘱𝘳𝘰𝘣𝘢𝘣𝘭𝘺 𝘩𝘢𝘷𝘦 𝘢 𝘴𝘪𝘨𝘯𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘮𝘢𝘺𝘣𝘦 𝘪𝘯 𝘌𝘶𝘳𝘰𝘱𝘦, 𝘢𝘯𝘥 𝘪𝘵’𝘴 𝘢 𝘨𝘳𝘦𝘢𝘵 𝘵𝘩𝘪𝘯𝘨. 𝘔𝘰𝘴𝘵 𝘪𝘮𝘱𝘰𝘳𝘵𝘢𝘯𝘵𝘭𝘺, 𝘸𝘦 𝘩𝘢𝘷𝘦 𝘢 𝘥𝘦𝘢𝘭 𝘵𝘩𝘢𝘵 𝘐𝘳𝘢𝘯 𝘸𝘪𝘭𝘭 𝘯𝘦𝘷𝘦𝘳 𝘩𝘢𝘷𝘦 𝘢 𝘯𝘶𝘤𝘭𝘦𝘢𝘳 𝘸𝘦𝘢𝘱𝘰𝘯.” — President Trump, June 11, 2026

Four U.S. Air Force C-17 transport aircraft departed for Europe overnight carrying advance equipment for a potential JD Vance trip to Geneva. Three CNN sources confirmed Geneva as the likely signing venue — possibly Sunday, June 14, ahead of the G7 summit. The deal is expected to be called the “Islamabad Declaration,” honoring Pakistan’s role as mediator.

Trump canceled a planned round of military strikes on Iran on June 11 after Iranian leadership approved the framework in principle. 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐧𝐚𝐯𝐚𝐥 𝐛𝐥𝐨𝐜𝐤𝐚𝐝𝐞 𝐨𝐟 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐒𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐢𝐭 𝐨𝐟 𝐇𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐮𝐳 𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐲𝐬 𝐢𝐧 𝐩𝐥𝐚𝐜𝐞 until the documents are signed. This morning, U.S. forces 𝐬∗𝐨𝐭 𝐝𝐨𝐰𝐧 𝐭𝐰𝐨 𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐈𝐫𝐚𝐧𝐢𝐚𝐧 𝐚𝐭𝐭𝐚𝐜𝐤 𝐝𝐫𝐨𝐧𝐞𝐬 near the Strait after Iran fired on a commercial vessel. Tehran is still testing edges while its diplomats are at the table.

What’s in the MOU: a 60-day ceasefire extension to allow nuclear talks to proceed. Iran commits to 𝐧𝐨 𝐧𝐮𝐜𝐥𝐞𝐚𝐫 𝐰𝐞𝐚𝐩𝐨𝐧𝐬 and to reopening the Strait within 30 days with no tolls and no harassment of shipping. The U.S. issues oil-sale waivers and lifts port restrictions; permanent sanctions relief follows only on verified Iranian performance. Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Qatar, and Israel were all consulted. Netanyahu stated publicly that Israel “is not a party” to the deal. Iran’s foreign ministry called the Geneva signing reports “merely speculation.”

𝐓𝐫𝐮𝐦𝐩 𝐝𝐢𝐝𝐧’𝐭 𝐠𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐚 𝐬𝐩𝐞𝐞𝐜𝐡 — 𝐡𝐞 𝐦𝐨𝐯𝐞𝐝 𝐟𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐂-𝟏𝟕𝐬 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐜𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞𝐥𝐞𝐝 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐢𝐤𝐞𝐬. 𝐈𝐫𝐚𝐧 𝐧𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫 𝐠𝐞𝐭𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐛𝐨𝐦𝐛, 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐛𝐥𝐨𝐜𝐤𝐚𝐝𝐞 𝐡𝐨𝐥𝐝𝐬 𝐮𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐥 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐭’𝐬 𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐞𝐝.

Grok - Is this latest peace "deal" Trump is working on with Iran an actual piece deal, or a memorandum of understanding. 
 
And if the latter, why is it's  duration ?   
 
---------------

It’s not a full peace deal—it’s a proposed (or tentatively reached) 60-day Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) to extend a ceasefire and launch further negotiations. https://t.co/llnxtRC3hB 
This is the “latest deal” Trump has been discussing in late May and into June 2026, amid the ongoing (or recently active) US-Iran conflict that escalated earlier in the year. It is not a comprehensive or permanent peace agreement. Instead, it serves as a short-term framework to pause hostilities, reopen key areas like the Strait of Hormuz for shipping/oil trade, and start talks on bigger issues (especially Iran’s nuclear program, highly enriched uranium stockpile, sanctions relief, etc.). https://t.co/llnxtRC3hB 
Key Details from Reports
•  U.S. and Iranian negotiators reached (or largely agreed on) the text of this MOU, but it has required Trump’s final approval (and Iran’s supreme leader’s buy-in). Trump has made edits for tougher terms (e.g., on nuclear commitments and the Strait of Hormuz) and has repeatedly described it as being in the “final throes,” “largely negotiated,” or imminent—sometimes claiming a signing could happen soon (e.g., in Europe). https://t.co/XaILuhztcg 
•  Iran has pushed back, with officials stating that nothing is finalized, calling some claims “speculative,” or noting excessive U.S. demands. There have been ongoing back-and-forths, occasional strikes, and skepticism from both sides (plus allies like Israel). https://t.co/Du2ztVq7d7 
•  As of the most recent reports (around June 11-12, 2026), Trump continues to express optimism that it’s close or done, while Iran has not confirmed acceptance. It remains fragile and subject to change. https://t.co/13S9E2vzJ4 
Duration
The MOU is explicitly designed to last 60 days (and can potentially be extended by mutual consent). During that window:
•  The ceasefire would be extended.
•  The Strait of Hormuz would reopen to unrestricted shipping (no tolls/mines blocking it).
•  Iran could sell oil more freely.
•  Negotiations would begin on a longer-term settlement, including nuclear curbs (e.g., no nuclear weapons, handling of enriched uranium, possible enrichment suspension), sanctions, and other issues. https://t.co/llnxtRC3hB 
In short, this is a temporary bridge to buy time for real talks, not the end of the conflict. Things have been fluid with claims of progress often followed by delays, edits, or renewed tensions. For the absolute latest status, I’d recommend checking reliable news outlets, as developments can shift quickly.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

------------

•Juli–September: Immer noch bullisch, solange der Hormus-Konflikt nicht gelöst ist. Preise könnten bei anhaltender Störung $100+ testen.
•Ab Herbst/Winter 2026: Mögliche Entspannung, wenn Verhandlungen Fortschritte machen und Lieferungen wieder anlaufen. EIA erwartet dann fallende Preise (durchschnittlich ~$95 für 2026 insgesamt, später tiefer).54
Fazit für deinen Short: Die nächsten 4–8 Wochen sind besonders gefährlich für Short-Positionen — hier könnte es teuer werden, wenn Inventories weiter schrumpfen. Danach hängt alles von geopolitischen Entwicklungen ab (Trump/Iran-Deals). Märkte sind volatil: Positive Nachrichten lassen Preise schnell fallen (wie kürzlich passiert).
Das ist kein Finanzrat — Märkte können unvorhersehbar reagieren. Überlege Stop-Loss, Hedging oder Positionsgröße anzupassen. Wenn du mehr Details zu Charts, spezifischen Kontrakten oder Alternativen brauchst, lass es mich wissen!

#OIL PRICES SLIDE OVER 4% TO TWO-MONTH LOW AS MIDDLE EAST TENSIONS EASE

🔹 Global crude oil prices fell sharply on June 12, 2026, with West Texas Intermediate Crude Oil (WTI) dropping more than 4% to a two-month low near $83 per barrel before recovering modestly toward $84.80.

🔹 Brent Crude Oil (Brent) also came under heavy selling pressure, slipping below the $87 per barrel mark as traders unwound geopolitical risk premiums.

🔹 The decline was driven primarily by signs of de-escalation in the Middle East, including the suspension of hostilities between Israel and Iran and growing optimism surrounding ongoing US-Iran diplomatic discussions.

🔹 Markets interpreted recent comments from Donald Trump and statements from Iranian officials as increasing the probability of a broader agreement that could stabilize regional energy flows.

🔹 The easing of tensions has erased a significant portion of the geopolitical premium that previously pushed crude oil prices above $100 per barrel during the height of regional uncertainty.

🔹 The move lower occurred despite supportive fundamental data, including recent draws in US crude inventories, indicating that geopolitical developments currently remain the dominant market driver.

🔹 Technical analysts note that crude oil has broken below several key support levels, with some market participants now watching the $78–$80 per barrel region as the next potential downside target if diplomatic momentum continues.

🔹 Lower crude prices are generally viewed as positive for oil-importing economies such as India, helping reduce inflationary pressures, improve trade balances, and support corporate margins across sectors including aviation, paints, chemicals, logistics, and consumer goods.

🔹 For Indian equities, sectors such as airlines, auto, FMCG, chemicals, and paints could benefit from softer energy costs, while upstream energy companies may face near-term pressure from weaker crude realizations.

🔹 Market Impact: Falling oil prices have strengthened risk sentiment globally, contributing to gains in equities, easing inflation concerns, and increasing expectations that central banks may gain greater flexibility on future policy decisions.

text:
📊 Summary of Oil Industry Movements - Week 24 | https://t.co/ZNGpg6vJQ0

China's Imports Collapse to an 8-Year Low
⚡ Reflecting the severe loss of seaborne Middle Eastern barrels, preliminary May data reveals that Chinese crude imports plunged to an eight-year low of just 7.79 MMBD—a level not seen since May 2017. Leaning on its massive domestic stockpiles and deep refinery run cuts to avoid bidding up hyper-expensive spot barrels, China’s 3-month import average has officially dropped below 10 MMBD. This massive retreat from the market is the primary anchor preventing a massive blowout in front-month global flat prices.  

LPG Shortages Force Severe Indian Demand Rationing
⚡ Combined Indian and regional naphtha demand hit multi-year lows as the reality of structural supply deprivation sinks in. India’s aggressive, government-backed demand conservation programs have been highly effective, particularly in rationing the country's starved LPG markets due to the absolute lack of flows from the Arabian Gulf. The primary debate now occupying the market is whether this forced demand destruction will become permanent or simply spring back once Hormuz eventually normalizes.

EIA Normalization Forecast Shifts from a "V" to a "U" Shape
⚡ The EIA’s latest June Short-Term Energy Outlook (STEO) has significantly grimmed its timeline for global oil recovery. Moving away from previous expectations of a quick rebound, the EIA has flattened its OPEC+ production restart curve from a sharp V-shape to a prolonged U-shape. Aggressive downward revisions have slashed projected July-to-November OPEC output by a massive -3.0 MMBD, pushing any semblance of supply normalization out to Q1 2027 and ensuring cumulative conflict-driven losses will eclipse 1.5 billion barrels.

US Inventories Bleed out at an Accelerated Clip
⚡ The operational pressure valve on the Western hemisphere is reaching a critical threshold. The EIA reported that US combined oil and product stocks fell by another -13 million barrels, with massive weekly draws becoming the structural norm as the Strategic Petroleum Reserve is systematically depleted. Commercial crude inventories are now sitting just 17 MMBBLs above their historical 2022 lows, while refined product cushions have been whittled down to within 55 MMBBLs of their absolute floor.

US Production Limits Tested Amid Global Shortage
⚡ The accelerating depletion of US inventories comes despite the domestic supply machine running at absolute maximum throttle. Compared to the 2022 energy crisis, the US is currently churning out 2.0 MMBD more crude oil while domestic refineries are processing 0.6 MMBD more feedstock. The fact that US inventories continue to collapse at an accelerating rate despite this massive incremental domestic supply highlights the insatiable, desperate global pull for US barrels.

With the EIA officially pushing out the timeline for a Hormuz resolution into 2027, would you like to take a closer look at how this U-shaped outlook is impacting the back-end pricing of the 2027/2028 Brent spreads?

text:
🔴WAR ON TERRORISM: IRAN 🇮🇷🔴/CTP-ISW/

The United States has also continued to enforce its blockade on Iranian ports as part of its economic pressure campaign on Iran.

CENTCOM announced that US forces disabled the Guinea-Bissau-flagged oil tanker M/V Jalveer on June 10 after the vessel attempted to violate the US blockade and transport Iranian oil outside of Iran.

The strike that disabled the vessel injured some crew members.

This is the third Iranian-linked vessel that US forces have disabled this week for failing to comply with the US blockade.

CENTCOM forces also disabled the Palau-flagged oil tanker M/T Settebello as it transited the Gulf of Oman on June 9 and disabled the Palau-flagged oil tanker M/T Marivex as it transited international waters in the Gulf of Oman toward Iran on June 8.

CENTCOM also announced on June 11 that US forces have disabled ”nine non-compliant vessels,” redirected 135 ships that attempted to run the US blockade, and allowed 42 vessels with humanitarian aid to transit since the United States initiated the blockade on April 13.

https://t.co/T9i1f1GkfE via @criticalthreats

Hormuz passage remains fragile

Transit activity through the Strait of Hormuz showed a modest day on day increase on 11 June, with six crossings recorded across the monitored zone. Traffic remained concentrated in non commercial movements, with commercial activity limited to a ballast oil tanker. Most vessels continued to follow the Iranian route, suggesting passage remains under tightly controlled conditions. At the same time, the security picture deteriorated further following an IMO verified attack on Jalveer and the addition of two earlier incidents to the record. The result is an operating environment that remains highly fragile. Diplomatic signals are equally mixed, with Washington and Tehran offering conflicting assessments of negotiations, leaving uncertainty over both the Strait’s security outlook and the prospects for a lasting agreement.

Oil dropping from $88 to $84 isn’t a “collapse.” That’s called volatility.

If oil was rising, we’d be told it’s because of global conflicts, OPEC, refinery outages, and market forces. When it falls, suddenly it’s one politician’s magic wand.

Oil is a global commodity traded by millions of buyers and sellers. Prices move on supply, demand, inventories, geopolitics, and trader expectations.

If politicians get credit every time oil drops, do they get blamed every time it rises?
